      "plan": {

parsed.goal: Comprehensive mastery from scratch; seeking an interactive, first-principles alternative to traditional reading.

modules.title: Rebuilding Mathematical Foundations: Sets, Relations, and Functions
modules.description: Addressing the 10-year gap in mathematics by starting at ground zero. This module rebuilds the vocabulary of discrete math used throughout CLRS, ensuring proofs later on feel intuitive rather than intimidating.

plan.goal: Define and manipulate sets, relations, functions, and basic graph theory components from first principles without relying on rote memorization.

modules.title: Combinatorics and Probability Primer
modules.description: A deep dive into counting and probability, which are non-negotiable for understanding the randomized algorithms and average-case analyses later in the book.

plan.goal: Calculate permutations, combinations, and expected values, and apply indicator random variables to basic probabilistic scenarios.

modules.title: The Pseudocode Paradigm and Loop Invariants
modules.description: Transitioning from math into computation. We dissect the CLRS pseudocode style and translate it into Python, using Insertion Sort as our vehicle for learning how to formally prove a program works.

plan.goal: Translate CLRS pseudocode into working Python and rigorously prove correctness using initialization, maintenance, and termination of loop invariants.

modules.title: Asymptotic Notation: The Language of Growth
modules.description: The absolute core of algorithmic analysis. We will define Big-O, Omega, and Theta strictly through mathematical limits and inequalities, leaving no ambiguity about what 'fast' means.

plan.goal: Formally prove the asymptotic bounds of given functions using the c and n0 constant definitions of Big-O, Omega, and Theta.

modules.title: Divide-and-Conquer and The Master Theorem
modules.description: Exploring recursive algorithm design. You will build recursion trees by hand and learn to apply the Master Theorem to solve recurrences instantly.

plan.goal: Solve algorithmic recurrences using substitution, recursion trees, and the Master Theorem, and implement a divide-and-conquer algorithm (Merge Sort or Maximum Subarray).
concepts: The Master method and its three cases

modules.title: Probabilistic Analysis and Randomized Algorithms
modules.description: Applying the probability foundations to algorithms. We evaluate how randomization can protect algorithms against worst-case inputs, using the Hiring Problem as a case study.

plan.goal: Differentiate between average-case and randomized algorithms, and use indicator random variables to calculate the expected running time of randomized processes.
concepts: Indicator random variables in algorithm analysis

modules.title: Heapsort and Priority Queues
modules.description: Our first major data structure. We will implement heaps on top of raw arrays, prove the mathematical heights of the tree nodes, and build a priority queue.

plan.goal: Implement Max-Heapify, Build-Max-Heap, and Heapsort in Python, and mathematically prove the O(n) bound for building a heap.
concepts: Building a heap in linear time
sourceRefs: Part II Sorting and Order Statistics

modules.title: Quicksort: Worst-Case vs Expected Time
modules.description: Quicksort is elegantly simple but mathematically nuanced. We will implement it, break it with worst-case inputs, and then fix it using randomization, proving its expected O(n log n) runtime.

plan.goal: Implement standard and randomized Quicksort, and perform a rigorous expected-time analysis using probability.
sourceRefs: Part II Sorting and Order Statistics

modules.title: Breaking the O(n log n) Barrier: Linear-Time Sorting
modules.description: Proving mathematically that comparison sorts have a hard speed limit, and then exploring how non-comparison sorts (Counting, Radix, Bucket) bypass this limit entirely.

plan.goal: Prove the Omega(n log n) lower bound for comparison sorting via decision trees, and implement Counting Sort and Radix Sort.
sourceRefs: Part II Sorting and Order Statistics

modules.title: Medians and Order Statistics
modules.description: How do you find the median of an unsorted array without sorting it? This module explores both expected linear-time and guaranteed linear-time selection algorithms.

plan.goal: Implement Randomized-Select and the deterministic Median-of-Medians algorithm, analyzing their running times.
concepts: Randomized selection in expected linear time
Deterministic selection in worst-case linear time (Median of Medians)
sourceRefs: Part II Sorting and Order Statistics
prerequisites: The Master method and its three cases

modules.title: Elementary Data Structures: Abstracting Memory
modules.description: Moving from arrays to dynamic memory. We build Stacks, Queues, and Linked Lists from scratch, focusing on pointer manipulation and the concept of abstract data types.

plan.goal: Implement stacks, queues, and doubly linked lists using array-backing and pointer/object representation.
concepts: Implementing pointers and objects in memory

modules.title: Hash Tables: Theory and Practice
modules.description: A deep dive into the mathematics of hashing. Moving beyond simple key-value pairs to mathematically prove the collision probabilities of universal hashing.

plan.goal: Design a hash table handling collisions via chaining and open addressing, and mathematically justify Universal Hashing.
concepts: Universal hashing and perfect hashing math

modules.title: Binary Search Trees
modules.description: Building dynamic sets. We will implement tree walks, insertions, deletions, and analyze how the tree's height entirely dictates operational time.

plan.goal: Implement a full Binary Search Tree (BST) class supporting traversal, search, min/max, predecessor/successor, insertion, and deletion.
concepts: Querying a BST (search, min, max, pred, succ)
Expected height of randomly built BSTs
prerequisites: Implementing pointers and objects in memory

modules.title: Red-Black Trees: Guaranteed Balance
modules.description: One of the most complex implementations in CLRS. We will rigorously define the 5 properties of Red-Black trees and map out every rotation and color-flip case.

plan.goal: Understand and trace the rebalancing logic of Red-Black Trees, proving how tree rotations preserve the O(log n) height guarantee.

modules.title: Dynamic Programming I: Optimization and Memoization
modules.description: Entering Part IV: Advanced Design. We shift from structures to strategies, learning to identify overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure using Rod Cutting and Matrix-Chain Multiplication.

plan.goal: Formulate dynamic programming recurrence relations and implement both top-down memoized and bottom-up solutions.
sourceRefs: Part IV Advanced Design and Analysis Techniques

modules.title: Dynamic Programming II: Advanced Applications
modules.description: Deepening our DP skills by exploring Longest Common Subsequence and Optimal Binary Search Trees, rigorously proving why the greedy approach fails here.

plan.goal: Design dynamic programming algorithms for strings and optimal trees, successfully tracing state-transition matrices.
sourceRefs: Part IV Advanced Design and Analysis Techniques

modules.title: Greedy Algorithms and Theoretical Matroids
modules.description: When does locally optimal mean globally optimal? We define the Greedy-Choice property, implement Huffman codes, and introduce the abstract algebra of Matroids.

plan.goal: Prove the greedy-choice property for an algorithm and implement Huffman coding, understanding the theoretical backing of Matroid theory.
concepts: Greedy-choice property vs DP optimal substructure
sourceRefs: Part IV Advanced Design and Analysis Techniques

modules.title: Amortized Analysis
modules.description: How to analyze data structures whose slow operations are rare enough that the average time per operation remains fast. Essential for understanding dynamic arrays (hash maps).

plan.goal: Perform amortized analysis using the aggregate method, accounting method, and potential method on dynamic tables and counters.
sourceRefs: Part IV Advanced Design and Analysis Techniques

modules.title: B-Trees: Managing Disk I/O
modules.description: Shifting to Part V: Advanced Data Structures. We explore B-trees, the backbone of modern databases, where minimizing disk reads matters more than raw CPU cycles.

plan.goal: Explain the disk I/O model and trace B-tree insertion/splitting algorithms.

modules.title: Data Structures for Disjoint Sets
modules.description: Implementing the Union-Find data structure. We study the elegance of path compression and union by rank, leading to the incredibly slow-growing inverse Ackermann function.

plan.goal: Implement a Union-Find data structure with path compression and rank heuristics, and conceptually grasp its nearly O(1) amortized bound.
concepts: Amortized bound of inverse Ackermann function O(α(n))

modules.title: Graph Algorithm Foundations: BFS and DFS
modules.description: Entering Part VI: Graph Algorithms. We establish representations (adjacency matrices vs lists) and master the foundational searches that power complex graph logic.

plan.goal: Represent graphs in code, execute Breadth-First and Depth-First searches, and use DFS to perform a Topological Sort.

modules.title: Minimum Spanning Trees
modules.description: Applying Greedy Algorithms and Disjoint Sets to Graphs. We implement Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms to find the cheapest way to connect networks.

plan.goal: Trace and implement Kruskal’s and Prim’s algorithms, proving their correctness via the generic MST cut property.
concepts: The cut property and safe edges
Time complexity analysis for sparse vs dense graphs
prerequisites: Greedy-choice property vs DP optimal substructure

modules.title: Single-Source Shortest Paths
modules.description: Beyond BFS. Handling weighted edges with Dijkstra's algorithm, and handling negative edge weights with the Bellman-Ford algorithm.

plan.goal: Implement Dijkstra's and Bellman-Ford algorithms, rigorously defining edge relaxation and proving correctness.

modules.title: All-Pairs Shortest Paths
modules.description: Applying Dynamic Programming to graph matrices. Exploring the Floyd-Warshall algorithm and Johnson's algorithm for sparse graphs.

plan.goal: Solve all-pairs shortest paths using matrix multiplication and the Floyd-Warshall DP approach.
concepts: Transitive closure of a directed graph

modules.title: Maximum Flow Networks
modules.description: A complex graph topic dealing with capacity. We prove the Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem and implement the Ford-Fulkerson method.

plan.goal: Model systems as flow networks, execute the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm using augmenting paths, and comprehend the max-flow min-cut theorem.

modules.title: Multithreaded Algorithms
modules.description: Entering Part VII: Selected Topics. A modern addition to algorithmic study: analyzing code that runs concurrently on multiple processors.

plan.goal: Analyze the theoretical work and span of parallel algorithms and implement multithreaded matrix multiplication or merge sort.

modules.title: Advanced Mathematics: Matrices, Polynomials, and FFT
modules.description: Bridging abstract algebra and algorithm design. We master the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT), a pivotal algorithm in signal processing and fast multiplication.

plan.goal: Understand polynomial representations, complex roots of unity, and trace the recursive Fast Fourier Transform algorithm.
concepts: Solving systems of linear equations (LUP decomposition)

modules.title: Number-Theoretic Algorithms and Cryptography
modules.description: The math behind modern security. Exploring modular arithmetic, prime finding, and the RSA public-key cryptosystem from first principles.

plan.goal: Compute greatest common divisors, trace modular exponentiation, and explain the mathematical proof of the RSA cryptosystem.

modules.title: String Matching Algorithms
modules.description: How text editors find substrings instantly. We move from the naive approach to Rabin-Karp hashing, and finally the elegant Knuth-Morris-Pratt automaton.

plan.goal: Implement String Matching algorithms and comprehend the state transitions in the KMP algorithm.

modules.title: Online Algorithms and Machine Learning
modules.description: A study of the 4th Edition's modern updates. How algorithms adapt when data streams in continually, and the algorithmic foundations of machine learning weights.

plan.goal: Explain the concept of competitive analysis for online algorithms and trace basic machine learning weight updates.

modules.title: NP-Completeness: The Limits of Efficient Computation
modules.description: The most important theoretical concept in computer science. What happens when problems are fundamentally too hard to solve perfectly? We define P vs NP and prove NP-completeness.

plan.goal: Define the complexity classes P, NP, and NP-Complete, and execute polynomial-time reductions between famous NP-Complete problems.

modules.title: Approximation Algorithms
modules.description: When a problem is NP-Complete, we compromise. We explore algorithms that guarantee a solution within a specific percentage of the true optimal answer.

plan.goal: Design and analyze approximation algorithms, proving their approximation ratios.

modules.title: Capstone: The First-Principles Algorithm Library
modules.description: The culmination of your 'from scratch' journey. Synthesize the textbook by finalizing a complete, cohesive library of the major algorithms and structures, entirely hand-coded, backed by your mathematical proofs.

plan.goal: Refactor, integrate, and test a comprehensive personal code library covering sorting, graph traversals, and dynamic programming algorithms from the curriculum.

modules.title: Rebuilding Mathematical Foundations: Sets, Relations, and Functions
The Pseudocode Paradigm and Loop Invariants
Asymptotic Notation: The Language of Growth
Breaking the O(n log n) Barrier: Linear-Time Sorting
Dynamic Programming I: Optimization and Memoization
Graph Algorithm Foundations: BFS and DFS
Advanced Mathematics: Matrices, Polynomials, and FFT
NP-Completeness: The Limits of Efficient Computation
